perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTI AL INFORMATION and to employ all reasonable efforts to maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as secret and Page 2 of 3 confidential, such efforts to be no less than the degree of care employed by RECIPIENT to preserve and safeguard RECIPIENT's own confidential informa tion.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTI AL INFORMATION and to employ efforts to maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as confidential. The degree of care will be considered during the project.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by removing the 'all reasonable efforts' requirement and replacing it with simply 'efforts'. The statement 'The degree of care will be considered during the project' adds further vagueness, making the obligation to protect confidential information unclear and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: The MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. By reducing the standard to merely 'efforts' and suggesting that the degree of care is only 'considered', the modified text contradicts the level of protection required under Maryland law to qualify for trade secret protection, potentially forfeiting legal recourse for misappropriation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203 (2022) - Right to Damages :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1203 - Right to Damages Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the requirement for protecting confidential information, potentially contradicting the standard required for trade secret protection. The original text mandates 'all reasonable efforts,' while the changed text only requires 'efforts' and vaguely mentions that the 'degree of care will be considered during the project.' This introduces ambiguity and reduces the level of protection. The Law Explanation specifies that MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, mentions damages for misappropriation, implying a need for adequate protection to be legally recognized. The reduced standard in the modified text could jeopardize legal recourse for misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will not use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for any purpose other than the PURPOSE without the prior written consent of PROVIDER.
perturbation.changed_text: However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will try not to use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for any purpose other than the PURPOSE without considering the PROVIDER's input.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not use...without prior written consent' with 'will try not to use...without considering the PROVIDER's input' significantly weakens the prohibition and introduces ambiguity. The 'try not to' makes compliance discretionary, and 'considering the PROVIDER's input' does not guarantee that the recipient will adhere to the PROVIDER's wishes or seek explicit consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Using the confidential information for other uses is considered misappropriation, in violation of the Act. By allowing the RECIPIENT to simply consider the PROVIDER's input, rather than obtaining written consent for usage beyond the defined purpose, this change undermines the protection of confidential information that the MUTSA seeks to provide.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 (2022) - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1202 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (e) In this section, “exceptional circumstances” includes a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating trade secret laws. The original text requires 'prior written consent,' while the changed text only requires 'considering the PROVIDER's input,' creating ambiguity and a discretionary obligation. According to the LLM explanation, this weakens the protection of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202, states that 'misappropriation may be enjoined', implying a legal standard for protecting trade secrets, which the changed text undermines. By weakening the requirement to obtain consent, the recipient may be able to misappropriate confidential information without explicitly violating the agreement, thus contradicting the intent of trade secret protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first not ify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to disclosing proprietary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT should consider notifying and offering an opportunity to prevent disclosure, if practical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall first notify and provide it an opportunity' to 'should consider notifying and offering an opportunity, if practical' weakens the obligation to notify and provide an opportunity to prevent disclosure. The addition of 'if practical' introduces a subjective element, allowing the RECIPIENT to decide whether or not to provide notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Act provides legal mechanisms to prevent the disclosure of confidential information. By making the notification process optional ('should consider') and adding the condition 'if practical,' the modified text contradicts the MUTSA's intent to provide robust protection and legal recourse to prevent misappropriation, as it allows the RECIPIENT to potentially bypass the notification requirement based on their subjective assessment of practicality.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205 (2022) - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1205 - Preservation of Trade Secret During Action Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1205 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In an action under this subtitle, a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. perturbation.justification: The change from "shall first notify and provide it an opportunity" to "should consider notifying and offering an opportunity, if practical" significantly weakens the obligation. This undermines the intent of laws designed to protect trade secrets, as explained by the LLM, because it allows the recipient to potentially bypass the notification requirement based on their subjective assessment of practicality. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not valid, Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1205, focuses on the 'Preservation of Trade Secret During Action' and states a court 'shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means'. By making notification optional, the modified text is less likely to allow for the secrecy to be preserved, thus contradicting the statute's intent.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 shall extend for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 should typically extend for approximately five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, unless otherwise deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly defines a five-year period for the RECIPIENT's obligations. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion with the phrases 'should typically extend,' 'approximately,' and 'unless otherwise deemed appropriate.' This weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations, as the duration is no longer clearly defined and can be altered based on undefined criteria.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the MUTSA doesn't specify a definite length of time, weakening the obligation undermines reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality. Allowing the RECIPIENT to subjectively determine the duration of confidentiality potentially renders the trade secret unprotected, as the law requires a continued effort to maintain secrecy. The 'unless otherwise deemed appropriate' clause provides no standard and contradicts the effort to protect confidential information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1201 - Definitions Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1201 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next (a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (c) “Misappropriation” means the: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person’s knowledge of the trade secret was: 1. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; 2. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of the person’s position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (d) “Person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, statutory trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or a
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of the RECIPIENT's obligations, weakening the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. The original text specifies a five-year period. The changed text uses terms like 'should typically extend,' 'approximately,' and 'unless otherwise deemed appropriate,' making the duration uncertain. The Law Explanation states that weakening the obligation undermines reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not useful, Scraped Law Snippet 2, from the Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By making the duration of the secrecy obligation unclear, the modified text increases the risk of a breach, thus contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets as outlined in the law. The phrase "unless otherwise deemed appropriate" introduces a subjective element that directly conflicts with maintaining a defined and enforceable duty of secrecy.
